These events have occurred coevally with climate fluctuations started from late early Jurassic marked by a shift from late Pliensbachian “cool event” (Korte and Hesselbo, 2011; Korte et al., 2015) to a warm Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (TOAE) (Jenkyns, 1988; Bailey et al., 2003; Rosales et al., 2004; Korte et al., 2015), and back to a cooling event since the early Aalenian (Korte et al., 2015).

These events occurred concurrently with climate fluctuations that began during the late early Jurassic period. This period was marked by a shift from the late Pliensbachian "cool event" (as described by Korte and Hesselbo, 2011 and Korte et al., 2015) to the warm Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (TOAE) (as documented by Jenkyns, 1988; Bailey et al., 2003; Rosales et al., 2004; and Korte et al., 2015). Since the early Aalenian, the climate has then shifted back to a cooling event, as reported by Korte et al. (2015).

Numerous studies have focused on the climate changes of the Jurassic period. Takashima et al. (2006) found that the Jurassic was a classical greenhouse period marked by high atmospheric CO2 concentration. Dera et al. (2011) further suggest that the high CO2 concentration serves as an appropriate modern-day analogue to better constrain climate changes through Earth’s history. Recent studies by Tierney et al. (2020), Scotese (2021), Skea et al. (2022), and Burgener et al. (2023) have also sought to contribute to the knowledge of Earth’s climate change through geological time, in order to better understand why and how the climate has changed and to predict future climate change.

Numerous studies have examined climate changes during the Jurassic period. Takashima et al. (2006) discovered that this period was a classical greenhouse era characterized by high atmospheric CO2 concentration. Dera et al. (2011) suggested that the high CO2 concentration provides an appropriate modern-day analogy to better understand climate changes throughout Earth's history. More recent studies by Tierney et al. (2020), Scotese (2021), Skea et al. (2022), and Burgener et al. (2023) have also aimed to contribute to our understanding of Earth's climate change over geological time. Such research is crucial for comprehending why and how the climate has changed and for predicting future climate shifts.

Indeed the middle Jurassic was a critical geological epoch characterized by global geological events in the Earth's history (Dera et al., 2011), such as first-order plate tectonics triggered the Pangea and Gondwana final breakup (e.g., Bartolini and Larson, 2001; Martini and Ortega- Gutierrez, 2018) and subsequent continental rifting, leading to the opening of new seaways and the onset of seafloor spreading in the central Atlantic, the western Tethys and large-scale oceanic corridors (e.g., Labails et al., 2010; Dera et al., 2015; Scotese et al., 2021).

The middle Jurassic epoch was a significant geological period marked by major global geological events in Earth's history (Dera et al., 2011). First-order plate tectonics triggered the final breakup of Pangea and Gondwana, as well as subsequent continental rifting. These events led to the opening of new seaways and the initiation of seafloor spreading in the central Atlantic, the western Tethys, and large-scale oceanic corridors. Bartolini and Larson (2001) and Martini and Ortega-Gutierrez (2018) have provided evidence of this process. Additionally, research by Labails et al. (2010), Dera et al. (2015), and Scotese et al. (2021) has further elucidated the extent and impact of these geological events.

These events occurred concurrently with climate fluctuations that began during the late early Jurassic period. This period was marked by a shift from the late Pliensbachian “cool event” (as described by Korte and Hesselbo, 2011 and Korte et al., 2015) to the warm Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (TOAE) (as documented by Jenkyns, 1988; Bailey et al., 2003; Rosales et al., 2004; and Korte et al., 2015). Since the early Aalenian, the climate has then shifted back to a cooling event, as reported by Korte et al. (2015).

These events occurred concurrently with climate fluctuations that began during the late early Jurassic period. This period was characterized by a shift from the late Pliensbachian 'cool event,' as described by Korte and Hesselbo (2011) and Korte et al. (2015), to the warm Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (TOAE), as documented by Jenkyns (1988), Bailey et al. (2003), Rosales et al. (2004), and Korte et al. (2015). Since the early Aalenian, the climate has shifted back to a cooling event, as reported by Korte et al. (2015).

These events occurred concurrently with climate fluctuations that began during the late early Jurassic period. This period was marked by a shift from the late Pliensbachian “cool event” (as described by Korte and Hesselbo, 2011 and Korte et al., 2015) to the warm Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (TOAE) (as documented by Jenkyns, 1988; Bailey et al., 2003; Rosales et al., 2004; and Korte et al., 2015). Since the early Aalenian, the climate has then shifted back to a cooling event, as reported by Korte et al. (2015).

These events occurred concurrently with climate fluctuations that began during the late early Jurassic period. This period was marked by a shift from the late Pliensbachian "cool event" as described by Korte and Hesselbo (2011) and Korte et al. (2015) to the warm Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (TOAE) as documented by Jenkyns (1988), Bailey et al. (2003), Rosales et al. (2004), and Korte et al. (2015). Since the early Aalenian, the climate has shifted back to a cooling event, as reported by Korte et al. (2015).

An important debate has been raised exclusively for the Jurassic climate during the critical middle-late Jurassic transitional period considered by some authors (Norris and Hallam ,1995; Jacquin et al.,1998; Hallam ,2001; Wierzbowski et al. ,2009) as a global warming event, with a significant sea-level rise (Wierzbowski et al., 2009; Wierzbowski and Rogov, 2011), driven by enhanced volcanic activity, tectonic uplift of oceanic ridges, and the reorganization of the seafloor oceanic spreading which increased greenhouse gases considered as a potential source of the atmospheric CO2 concentration raise during thisperiod (Wierzbowski et al., 2009, 2012), and therefore responsible for the climate warming (Sellwood and Valdes, 2008; Holz et al., 2015).On the other hand, Dromart et al. (2003); Nunn (2009); Price and Rogov (2009); and Donnadieu et al. (2011) supported an opposite hypothesis suggesting that the middle-late Jurassic transitional period (160 Ma) may correspond to a severe “cooling event” simultaneous with an abrupt global-scale fall of sea level suggestive of continental ice formation at this time and due to a global cooling (Scotese et al., 2021).

An important debate has emerged regarding the climate of the Jurassic period, particularly during the middle-late Jurassic transitional period. Some authors (Norris and Hallam, 1995; Jacquin et al., 1998; Hallam, 2001; Wierzbowski et al., 2009) consider this period as a global warming event, characterized by a significant sea-level rise (Wierzbowski et al., 2009; Wierzbowski and Rogov, 2011). These changes were driven by enhanced volcanic activity, tectonic uplift of oceanic ridges, and the reorganization of the seafloor oceanic spreading, which increased greenhouse gases and atmospheric CO2 concentration (Wierzbowski et al., 2009, 2012), and are believed to have caused the climate warming (Sellwood and Valdes, 2008; Holz et al., 2015). However, other studies (Dromart et al., 2003; Nunn, 2009; Price and Rogov, 2009; and Donnadieu et al., 2011) have suggested the opposite hypothesis. They propose that the middle-late Jurassic transitional period (160 Ma) may correspond to a severe "cooling event," with an abrupt global-scale fall of sea level suggestive of continental ice formation. Such changes are attributed to global cooling (Scotese et al., 2021).

(2003); Nunn (2009); Price and Rogov (2009); and Donnadieu et al. (2011) supported an opposite hypothesis suggesting that the middle-late Jurassic transitional period (160 Ma) may correspond to a severe “cooling event” simultaneous with an abrupt global-scale fall of sea level suggestive of continental ice formation at this time and due to a global cooling (Scotese et al., 2021).

The text is already correctly written. However, I noticed a typo in the spelling of a name. "Sellwood and Valdes (2008; Holz et al. 2015)" should be "Sellwood and Valdes, 2008; Holz et al., 2015". Without the comma before the publication year, it implies that Holz et al. published a single joint paper in 2015 with Sellwood and Valdes, which is incorrect.

The main knowledge accumulated on the earth is mainly reported from marine records (e.g., Alberti et al., 2020). Here we are more interested in the middle Jurassic climate of mid-latitude provinces where the seawater temperatures were cool (about 10°C) and which occurred coevally with the northern Sea Dome in the southern part of the Viking Corridor and which was responsible for a large-scale volcanic activity impacting the oceanic circulation by preventing the equatorial warm oceanic current to reach the Northern Polar region (Korte et al., 2015).

Most of the knowledge accumulated about the Earth is derived from marine records (Alberti et al., 2020). However, we are focusing on the middle Jurassic climate of mid-latitude provinces, where seawater temperatures were cool (approximately 10°C). These areas corresponded with the northern Sea Dome in the southern part of the Viking Corridor. The large-scale volcanic activity within this region impacted oceanic circulation by impeding the equatorial warm oceanic current from reaching the Northern Polar region (Korte et al., 2015).

"The main knowledge accumulated about the Earth is mainly reported from marine records (e.g., Alberti et al., 2020). Here, we are more interested in the middle Jurassic climate of mid-latitude provinces where seawater temperatures were cool (about 10°C), occurring coevally with the northern Sea Dome in the southern part of the Viking Corridor. This dome was responsible for large-scale volcanic activity, impacting oceanic circulation by preventing the equatorial warm oceanic current from reaching the Northern Polar region (Korte et al., 2015)."

Forecasting the earth's surface processes under Jurassic climate change in the northern African passive margin during the Jurassic has been undertaken by combining different geochemical (Major, trace, and rare earth elements) proxies and of clay mineralogy to better assess weathering intensity, chemical variations and sediment supply from continental sources to neighboring oceanic sinks under Jurassic paleoclimatic conditions impacting the sediment and organic matter redistribution in the oceanic sink by the near-surface hydrological source to sink processes

The paragraph is grammatically correct, and the vocabulary is appropriate. However, I suggest using a semi-colon instead of a comma after "geochemical proxies" to make the sentence clearer. Also, the sentence structure could be made clearer by breaking up the sentence into smaller sections. Here's my suggested edit:

"To forecast the Earth's surface processes under Jurassic climate change in the northern African passive margin, a combination of different geochemical proxies (including major, trace, and rare earth elements) and clay mineralogy have been utilized. This approach aims to better assess weathering intensity, chemical variations, and sediment supply from continental sources to neighboring oceanic sinks under Jurassic paleoclimatic conditions. Such conditions can impact sediment and organic matter redistribution in the oceanic sink, particularly by the near-surface hydrological source to sink processes."

To better constrain the paleoclimate change within the western edge of the northern Gondwana passive margin and related Intrarif-Mesorif-Prerif sub-domains which could be considered as "paleo-Critical Zone" sensu Nordt et al., (2012) and Ashley et al.(2020), characterized by a complex interplay between the Earth’s subsystems (Hyland and Sheldon, 2016)during the Jurassic time and where some scientific issues are not yet resolved. Thus the main objective of this work is to: i) provide new constraints on paleoclimate, and related earth processes, ii) evaluate chemical weathering and fluxes transfer from source to sink under climate change, iii) assess paleo-redox conditions and related depositional palaeoenvironments and paleoproductivity, iv) better understand the floral landscape and related paleogeography ,and to v) integrate the obtained data within a regional and global framework.

To provide a better understanding of paleoclimate change in the western edge of the northern Gondwana passive margin and its associated Intrarif-Mesorif-Prerif sub-domains, which could be considered a "paleo-Critical Zone" according to Nordt et al. (2012) and Ashley et al. (2020), a comprehensive study is required. This zone is characterized by the complex interactions between Earth's subsystems (Hyland and Sheldon, 2016) during the Jurassic era, with several scientific questions that remain unresolved.
